# Optionality Audit Brief — Sample Coach, Q2 2026 (Apr 1 – Jun 30)

*Private. Candid. Not for sharing. This is a sample brief used for testing the quarterly ingestion path.*

Sources: 13 weekly rollups, project-tracker.md PR record, content-log.md, redflags-log.md.

---

## 1. Effort distribution across the quarter

**Quarter average across 13 weeks:** Product 25% · Solution Engineering 30% · Engineering 25% · Customer Success 11% · Other 9%.

The distribution held roughly steady across the quarter, with a brief spike in Solution Engineering during a heavy onboarding stretch in May, and Product recovering to its baseline share by the back half of June.

---

## 2. What product work actually got done

- **Cash management page** — owned from concept through design review and shipped to general availability.
- **Customer pain-point synthesis** — synthesized recurring themes from customer conversations into a shared pain-point map, shared with the team.
- **Planning-cadence reform** — proposed and landed a change to the team's prioritization cadence.
- **Forecasting methodology** — built a first-pass statistical model for a recurring customer request, tested live with two accounts.

---

## 3. Publishing trajectory

No public posts this quarter. The "publish something" action item was carried for several weeks running before being retired at quarter end in favor of a delegated marketing motion.

---

## 4. Customer-contact cadence

Cadence stayed healthy throughout — the no-customer-call flag never fired this quarter. Several weeks included five or more external calls.

---

## 5. Aggregated behavioural pattern

Consistently collaborative and direct. Comfortable conceding a point when challenged with good evidence. No interpersonal friction observed. One recurring watch item: total meeting load trending high without corresponding delegation.

---

## 6. Summary verdict (private)

A strong quarter of execution, mostly expressed through solution-engineering and delivery work rather than dedicated product-discovery time. The publishing gap remains the clearest opportunity for external visibility next quarter.
